    • generalizing to new examples becomes exponentially more difficult when working with high-dimensional data, and
    • how the mechanisms used to achieve generalization in traditional machine learning are insufficient to learn complicated functions in high-dimensional spaces.

5.11.1 The Curse of Dimensionality

  • Many machine learning problems become exceedingly difficult when the number of dimensions in the data is high.
  • Many traditional machine learning algorithms simply assume that the output at a new point should be approximately the same as the output at the nearest training point.

5.11.2 Local Constancy and Smoothness Regularization 局部不变性和平滑正则化

  • To generalize well, machine learning algorithms need to be guided by prior beliefs about what kind of function they should learn.
  • Among the most widely used of these implicit “priors” is the smoothness prior , or local constancy prior .
    • This prior states that the function we learn should not change very much within a small region.
    • they fail to scale to the statistical challenges involved in solving AI-level tasks.
  • The smoothness assumption and the associated nonparametric learning algorithms work extremely well as long as there are enough examples for the learning algorithm to observe high points on most peaks and low points on most valleys of the true underlying function to be learned.
  • The key insight is that a very large number of regions, such as $O\left(2^{k}\right)$ can be defined with $O(k)$ examples, so long as we introduce some dependencies between the regions through additional assumptions about the underlying data-generating distribution.
  • The core idea in deep learning is that we assume that the data was generated by the composition of factors, or features, potentially at multiple levels in a hierarchy.

5.11.3 Manifold Learning

  • A manifold is a connected region.
    • Mathematically, it is a set of points associated with a neighborhood around each point. From any given point, the manifold locally appears to be a Euclidean space. In everyday life, we experience the surface of the world as a 2-D plane, but it is in fact a spherical manifold in 3-D space.
  • Many machine learning problems seem hopeless if we expect the machine learning algorithm to learn functions with interesting variations across all of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.
  • Manifold learning algorithms surmount this obstacle by assuming that most of $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ consists of invalid inputs, and that interesting inputs occur only along a collection of manifolds containing a small subset of points, with interesting variations in the output of the learned function occurring only along directions that lie on the manifold, or with interesting variations happening only when we move from one manifold to another.
  • We argue that in the context of AI tasks, such as those that involve processing images, sounds, or text, the manifold assumption is at least approximately correct.
    • The first observation in favor of the manifold hypothesis is that the probability distribution over images, text strings, and sounds that occur in real life is highly concentrated.
      • We must also establish that the examples we encounter are connected to each other by other examples, with each example surrounded by other highly similar examples that can be reached by applying transformations to traverse the manifold.
    • The second argument in favor of the manifold hypothesis is that we can imagine such neighborhoods and transformations, at least informally.
  • When the data lies on a low-dimensional manifold, it can be most natural for machine learning algorithms to represent the data in terms of coordinates on the manifold, rather than in terms of coordinates in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ .
entry.summary: The notes of deep learning book. Chapter 5.

entry.title: DL-book 4 Numerical Computation
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

4.1 Overﬂow and Underﬂow

  • The fundamental difficulty in performing continuous math on a digital computer is that we need to represent infinitely many real numbers with a finite number of bit patterns.
    • we incur some approximation error
      • In many cases, this is just rounding error 舍入误差
        • can cause algorithms that work in theory to fail in practice
  • Underflow 下溢 occurs when numbers near zero are rounded to zero.
    • e.g. divided by zero
  • Overflow 上溢 occurs when numbers with large magnitude are approximated as $\infty$ or $-\infty$.
  • One example of a function that must be stabilized against underflow and overflow is the softmax function, which is often used to predict the probabilities associated with a multinoulli distribution.
    • $\operatorname{softmax}( oldsymbol{x})_{i}= rac{\exp \left(x_{i} ight)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \exp \left(x_{j} ight)}$
    • Both of these difficulties can be resolved by instead evaluating $\operatorname{softmax}( oldsymbol{z})$ where $ oldsymbol{z}= oldsymbol{x}-\max _{i} x_{i}$.
      • Subtracting $\max _{i} x_{i}$ results in the largest argument to exp being $0$, which rules out the possibility of overflow.
      • Likewise, at least one term in the denominator has a value of $1$, which rules out the possibility of underflow in the denominator leading to a division by zero.
  • Developers of low-level libraries should keep numerical issues in mind when implementing deep learning algorithms.

4.2 Poor Conditioning

  • Conditioning refers to how rapidly a function changes with respect to small changes in its inputs.
    • rounding errors in the inputs can result in large changes in the output.
  • Consider the function $f( oldsymbol{x})= oldsymbol{A}^{-1} oldsymbol{x}$. When $ oldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes n}$ has an eigenvalue decomposition, its condition number is
    • $\max _{i, j}\left| rac{\lambda_{i}}{\lambda_{j}} ight|$
    • When this number is large, matrix inversion is particularly sensitive to error in the input.

4.3 Gradient-Based Optimization

  • We usually phrase most optimization problems in terms of minimizing $f(x)$.
  • The function we want to minimize or maximize is called the objective function , or criterion . When we are minimizing it, we may also call it the cost function, loss function , or error function .
  • We often denote the value that minimizes or maximizes a function with a superscript $∗$.
    • $ oldsymbol{x}^{*}=\arg \min f( oldsymbol{x})$
  • Suppose we have a function $y = f(x )$
    • derivative, $f^{\prime}(x)$ or as $ rac{d y}{d x}$ gives the slope of $ f(x )$ at the point $x$
      • it specifies how to scale a small change in the input to obtain the corresponding change in the output
      • $f(x+\epsilon) \approx f(x)+\epsilon f^{\prime}(x)$
    • gradient descent， We can thus reduce $f(x )$ by moving $x$ in small steps with the opposite sign of the derivative.
    • Points where $f^{\prime}(x)=0$ are known as critical points , or stationary points .
    • A local minimum is a point where $f(x )$ is lower than at all neighboring points, so it is no longer possible to decrease $f(x )$ by making infinitesimal steps.
      • vice versa, local maximum
      • or a saddle point, which has neighbors that are both higher and lower than the point itself.
    • A point that obtains the absolute lowest value of $f(x )$ is a global minimum.
    • The gradient generalizes the notion of derivative to the case where the derivative is with respect to a vector:
      • the gradient of $f$ is the vector containing all the partial derivatives, denoted $ abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f( oldsymbol{x})$
        • Element $i$ of the gradient is the partial derivative of $f$ with respect to $x_i$.
      • The directional derivative in direction $ oldsymbol{u}$ (a unit vector) is the slope of the function $f$ in direction $ oldsymbol{u}$ .
        • $ rac{\partial}{\partial \alpha} f( oldsymbol{x}+\alpha oldsymbol{u}) = oldsymbol{u}^{ op} abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f( oldsymbol{x})$, when $\alpha= 0$.
    • To minimize $f$ , we would like to find the direction in which $f$ decreases the fastest.
      • $\min _{ oldsymbol{u}, oldsymbol{u}^{ op} oldsymbol{u}=1} oldsymbol{u}^{ op} abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f( oldsymbol{x}) = \min _{ oldsymbol{u}, oldsymbol{u}^{ op} oldsymbol{u}=1}| oldsymbol{u}|_{2}\left| abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f( oldsymbol{x}) ight|_{2} \cos heta$
      • Substituting in $| oldsymbol{u}|_{2}=1$ and ignoring factors that do not depend on $ oldsymbol{u}$, this simplifies to
        • $\min _{ oldsymbol{u}} \cos heta$
        • This is minimized when $ oldsymbol{u}$ points in the opposite direction as the gradient.
          • In other words, the gradient points directly uphill, and the negative gradient points directly downhill.
      • This is known as the method of steepest descent, or gradient descent.
    • Steepest descent proposes a new point
      • $ oldsymbol{x}^{\prime}= oldsymbol{x}-\epsilon abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f( oldsymbol{x})$
        • where $\epsilon$ is the learning rate
    • Steepest descent converges when every element of the gradient is zero (or, inpractice, very close to zero).
    • Ascending an objective function of discrete parameters is called hill climbing.

4.3.1 Beyond the Gradient: Jacobian and Hessian Matrices

  • Jacobian matrix： Sometimes we need to find all the partial derivatives of a function whose input and output are both vectors. The matrix containing all such partial derivatives is known as a Jacobian matrix.
    • Specifically, if we have a function $ oldsymbol{f} : \mathbb{R}^{m} ightarrow \mathbb{R}^{n}$, then the Jacobian matrix $ oldsymbol{J} \in \mathbb{R}^{n imes m} $ of $ oldsymbol{f}$ is defined such that
      • $J_{i, j}= rac{\partial}{\partial x_{j}} f( oldsymbol{x})_{i}$
    • second derivative: a derivative of a derivative
      • $ rac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x_{i} \partial x_{j}} f$
      • In a single dimension
        • $ rac{d^{2}}{d x^{2}} f$ by $f^{\prime \prime}(x)$
      • We can think of the second derivative as measuring curvature .
        • With negative curvature, the cost function actually decreases faster than the gradient predicts.
        • With no curvature, the gradient predicts the decrease correctly.
        • With positive curvature, the function decreases more slowly than expected and eventually begins to increase, so steps that are too large can actually increase the function inadvertently.
  • Hessian matrix: When our function has multiple input dimensions, there are many second derivatives.
    • $ oldsymbol{H}(f)( oldsymbol{x})_{i, j}= rac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x_{i} \partial x_{j}} f( oldsymbol{x})$
    • Equivalently, the Hessian is the Jacobian of the gradient.
    • Because the Hessian matrix is real and symmetric, we can decompose it into a set of real eigenvalues and an orthogonal basis of eigenvectors.
      • The maximum eigenvalue determines the maximum second derivative, and the minimum eigenvalue determines the minimum second derivative.
    • The (directional) second derivative tells us how well we can expect a gradient descent step to perform.
    • We can make a second-order Taylor series approximation to the function $f( oldsymbol{x})$ aroungd the current point $ oldsymbol{x}^{(0)}$:
      • $f( oldsymbol{x}) \approx f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)+\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)^{ op} oldsymbol{g}+ rac{1}{2}\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)^{ op} oldsymbol{H}\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)$
      • with learning rate $\epsilon$
      • $f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)}-\epsilon oldsymbol{g} ight) \approx f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)-\epsilon oldsymbol{g}^{ op} oldsymbol{g}+ rac{1}{2} \epsilon^{2} oldsymbol{g}^{ op} oldsymbol{H} oldsymbol{g}$
      • There are three terms here: the original value of the function, the expected improvement due to the slope of the function, and the correction we must apply to account for the curvature of the function.
      • When $ oldsymbol{g}^{ op} oldsymbol{H} oldsymbol{g}$ is positive, the optimal stepsoze yields
        • $\epsilon^{*}= rac{ oldsymbol{g}^{ op} oldsymbol{g}}{ oldsymbol{g}^{ op} oldsymbol{H} oldsymbol{g}}$
        • worst case, optimal step is given by $ rac{1}{\lambda_{\max }}$,determined by the eigenvalues of the Hessian
    • In multiple dimensions, the condition number of the Hessian at this point measures how much the second derivatives differ from each other.
      • When the Hessian has a poor condition number, gradient descent performs poorly.
      • Poor condition number also makes choosing a good step size difficult.
  • Newton’s method, using information from the Hessian matrix to guide the search
    • is based on using a second-order Taylor series expansion to approximate $f( oldsymbol{x}) $ near some point $ oldsymbol{x}^{(0)}$ :
      • $f( oldsymbol{x}) \approx f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)+\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)^{ op} abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)+ rac{1}{2}\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)^{ op} oldsymbol{H}(f)\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)\left( oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)$
      • $ oldsymbol{x}^{*}= oldsymbol{x}^{(0)}- oldsymbol{H}(f)\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)^{-1} abla_{ oldsymbol{x}} f\left( oldsymbol{x}^{(0)} ight)$
      • When $f$ is a positive definite quadratic function, Newton’s method consists of applying equation once to jump to the minimum of the function directly.
      • Only for PD Hessian: Iteratively updating the approximation and jumping to the minimum of the approximation can reach the critical point much faster than gradient descent would.
        • This is a useful property near a local minimum, but it can be a harmful property near a saddle point, whereas gradient descent is not attracted to saddle points unless the gradient points toward them.
  • First-order optimization algorithms, optimization algorithms that use only the gradient, such as gradient descent
  • Second-order optimization algorithms, optimization algorithms that also use the Hessian matrix, such as Newton’s method
  • Lipschitz continuous
    • A Lipschitz continuous function is a function $f$ whose rate of change is bounded by a Lipschitz constant $\mathcal{L}$:
    • $ orall oldsymbol{x}, orall oldsymbol{y},|f( oldsymbol{x})-f( oldsymbol{y})| \leq \mathcal{L}| oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{y}|_{2}$
    • quantify our assumption that a small change in the input made by an algorithm will have a small change in the output.

4.4 Constrained Optimization

  • constrained optimization: we may wish to find the maximal or minimal value of $f( oldsymbol{x})$ for values of $ oldsymbol{x}$ in some set $\mathbb{S}$ .
  • Points $ oldsymbol{x}$ that lie within the set $\mathbb{S}$ are called feasible points 可行点 in constrained optimization terminology.
  • When possible, this method can be made more efficient by projecting the gradient into the tangent space of the feasible region before taking the step or beginning the line search.
  • The Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) approach provides a very general solution to constrained optimization.
    • we introduce a new function called the generalized Lagrangian or generalized Lagrange function.
      • $L( oldsymbol{x}, oldsymbol{\lambda}, oldsymbol{\alpha})=f( oldsymbol{x})+\sum_{i} \lambda_{i} g^{(i)}( oldsymbol{x})+\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} h^{(j)}( oldsymbol{x})$
    • and $\mathbb{S}=\lbrace oldsymbol{x} | orall i, g^{(i)}( oldsymbol{x})=0 ext { and } orall j, h^{(j)}( oldsymbol{x}) \leq 0 brace$ with equality constraints $g^{(j)}$ and inequality constraints $h^{(j)}$
    • then
      • $\min _{ oldsymbol{x}} \max _{ oldsymbol{\lambda}} \max _{ oldsymbol{\alpha}, oldsymbol{\alpha} \geq 0} L( oldsymbol{x}, oldsymbol{\lambda}, oldsymbol{\alpha})$
    • has the same optimal objective function value and set of optimal points $ oldsymbol{x}$ as
      • $\min _{ oldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{S}} f( oldsymbol{x})$
    • This follows because any time the constraints are satisfied,
      • $\max _{\lambda} \max _{ oldsymbol{\alpha}, oldsymbol{\alpha} \geq 0} L( oldsymbol{x}, oldsymbol{\lambda}, oldsymbol{\alpha})=f( oldsymbol{x})$
    • while any time a constraint is violated,
      • $\max _{ oldsymbol{\lambda}} \max _{ oldsymbol{\alpha}, oldsymbol{\alpha} \geq 0} L( oldsymbol{x}, oldsymbol{\lambda}, oldsymbol{\alpha})=\infty$
    • These properties guarantee that no infeasible point can be optimal, and that the optimum within the feasible points is unchanged.
  • KKT conditions
    • The gradient of the generalized Lagrangian is zero.
    • All constraints on both $ oldsymbol{x}$ and the KKT multipliers are satisfied.
    • The inequality constraints exhibit “complementary slackness”: $ oldsymbol{\alpha} \odot oldsymbol{h}( oldsymbol{x})=\mathbf{0}$.

4.5 Example: Linear Least Squares

  • $f( oldsymbol{x})= rac{1}{2}| oldsymbol{A} oldsymbol{x}- oldsymbol{b}|_{2}^{2}$
    • Gradient descent
    • Newton’s method
  • suppose subject to the constraint $ oldsymbol{x}^{ op} oldsymbol{x} \leq 1$
    • we introduce the Lagrangian
      • $L( oldsymbol{x}, \lambda)=f( oldsymbol{x})+\lambda\left( oldsymbol{x}^{ op} oldsymbol{x}-1 ight)$
      • $\min _{ oldsymbol{x}} \max _{\lambda, \lambda \geq 0} L( oldsymbol{x}, \lambda)$
        • We can find this value by performing gradient ascent on $\lambda$. To do so, observe
        • $ rac{\partial}{\partial \lambda} L( oldsymbol{x}, \lambda)= oldsymbol{x}^{ op} oldsymbol{x}-1$
entry.summary: The notes of deep learning book. Chapter 4.

entry.title: DL-book 3 Probability and Information Theory
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

  • in two major ways
    • First, the laws of probability tell us how AI systems should reason, so we design our algorithms to compute or approximate various expressions derived using probability theory.
    • Second, we can use probability and statistics to theoretically analyze the behavior of proposed AI systems.

3.1 Why Probability?

  • Machine learning must always deal with uncertain quantities and sometimes stochastic (nondeterministic) quantities.
  • three possible sources of uncertainty:
    • Inherent stochasticity in the system being modeled. probabilistic
    • Incomplete observability. cannot observe all the variables
    • Incomplete modeling. must discard some of the information
  • The former kind of probability, related directly to the rates at which events occur, is known as frequentist probability,
    • while the latter, related to qualitative levels of certainty, is known as Bayesian probability.
  • Probability can be seen as the extension of logic to deal with uncertainty.
  • Probability theory provides a set of formal rules for determining the likelihood of a proposition being true given the likelihood of other propositions.

3.2 Random Variables

  • plain typeface： x
  • the values it can take on with lowercase script letters：$x_1$

3.3 Probability Distributions

  • A probability distribution is a description of how likely a random variable or set of random variables is to take on each of its possible states.

3.3.1 Discrete Variables and Probability Mass Functions

  • A probability distribution over discrete variables may be described using a probability mass function (PMF).
    • $P(\mathrm{x}=x)$
    • $\mathrm{x}\sim P(\mathrm{x})$
  • joint probability distribution
    • $P(\mathrm{x}=x, \mathrm{y}=y)$
  • normalized
    • $\sum_{x \in \mathrm{x}} P(x)=1$
  • uniform distribution
    • $P\left(\mathrm{x}=x_{i}\right)=\frac{1}{k}, \forall i$

3.3.2 Continuous Variables and Probability Density Functions

  • When working with continuous random variables, we describe probability distributions using a probability density function (PDF).
  • Note that we do not require $p(x) \leq 1$
  • A probability density function $p(x)$ does not give the probability of a specific state directly; instead the probability of landing inside an infinitesimal region with volume $\delta x$ is given by $p(x)\delta x$
  • uniform distribution
    • $u(x ; a, b)=\frac{1}{b-a}$
    • $\mathrm{x} \sim U(a, b)$

3.4 Marginal Probability 边缘概率

  • joint probability distribution. The probability distribution over the subset is known as the marginal probability distribution.
    • We can find $\mathrm{x}$ with the sum rule:
      • $\forall x \in \mathrm{x}, P(\mathrm{x}=x)=\sum_y P(\mathrm{x}=x, \mathrm{y}=y)$
      • $p(x)=\int p(x, y) d y$

3.5 Conditional Probability

  • $P(\mathrm{y}=y | \mathrm{x}=x)=\frac{P(\mathrm{y}=y, \mathrm{x}=x)}{P(\mathrm{x}=x)}$

3.6 The Chain Rule of Conditional Probabilities

  • Any joint probability distribution over many random variables may be decomposed into conditional distributions over only one variable:
    • $P\left(\mathrm{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathrm{x}^{(n)}\right)=P\left(\mathrm{x}^{(1)}\right) \Pi_{i=2}^{n} P\left(\mathrm{x}^{(i)} | \mathrm{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \mathrm{x}^{(i-1)}\right)$

3.7 Independence and Conditional Independence

  • Two random variables x and y are independent if their probability distribution can be expressed as a product of two factors, one involving only x and one involving only y:
    • $\forall x \in \mathrm{x}, y \in \mathrm{y}, p(\mathrm{x}=x, \mathrm{y}=y)=p(\mathrm{x}=x) p(\mathrm{y}=y)$
    • $\mathrm{x} \perp \mathrm{y}$
  • conditionally independent
    • $\forall x \in \mathrm{x}, y \in \mathrm{y}, z \in \mathrm{z}, p(\mathrm{x}=x, \mathrm{y}=y | \mathrm{z}=z)=p(\mathrm{x}=x | \mathrm{z}=z) p(\mathrm{y}=y | \mathrm{z}=z)$
    • $\mathrm{x} \perp \mathrm{y} | \mathrm{z}$

3.8 Expectation, Variance and Covariance

  • The expectation , or expected value, of some function $f(x)$ with respect to a probability distribution $P(\mathrm{x})$ is the average, or mean value, that $f$ takes on when $x$ is drawn from $P$ .
    • discrete variables
      • $\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x} \sim P}[f(x)]=\sum_{x} P(x) f(x)$
    • continuous variables
      • $\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim p}[f(x)]=\int p(x) f(x) d x$
  • The variance gives a measure of how much the values of a function of a random variable $x$ vary as we sample different values of x from its probability distribution:
    • $\operatorname{Var}(f(x))=\mathbb{E}\left[(f(x)-\mathbb{E}[f(x)])^{2}\right]$
  • The square root of the variance is known as the standard deviation.
  • The covariance gives some sense of how much two values are linearly related to each other, as well as the scale of these variables:
    • covariance is for different dimensions (columns)
    • $\operatorname{Cov}(f(x), g(y))=\mathbb{E}[(f(x)-\mathbb{E}[f(x)])(g(y)-\mathbb{E}[g(y)])]$
    • Correlation normalize the contribution of each variable in order to measure only how much the variables are related.
    • Independence, however, is a distinct property from covariance.
      • nonlinear relationships
  • covariance matrix
    • $\operatorname{Cov}(\mathbf{x})_{i, j}=\operatorname{Cov}\left(\mathrm{x}_{i}, \mathrm{x}_{j}\right)$
    • The diagonal elements of the covariance give the variance:
      • $\operatorname{Cov}\left(\mathrm{x}_{i}, \mathrm{x}_{i}\right)=\operatorname{Var}\left(\mathrm{x}_{i}\right)$
    • $\operatorname{cov}(\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y})=\frac{\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\mathrm{n}}\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{X}}\right)\left(\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{Y}}\right)}{\mathrm{n}-1}$

3.9 Common Probability Distributions

3.9.1 Bernoulli Distribution

  • The Bernoulli distribution is a distribution over a single binary random variable.
  • $P(\mathrm{x}=1)=\phi$
  • $P(\mathrm{x}=0)=1-\phi$
  • $P(\mathrm{x}=x)=\phi^{x}(1-\phi)^{1-x}$
  • $\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{x}]=\phi$
  • $\mathrm{Var}_{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x})=\phi(1-\phi)$

3.9.2 Multinoulli Distribution

  • The multinoulli , or categorical 范畴, distribution is a distribution over a single discrete variable with $k$ different states, where $k$ is finite.

3.9.3 Gaussian Distribution

  • The most commonly used distribution over real numbers is the normal distribution, also known as the Gaussian distribution:
    • $\mathcal{N}\left(x ; \mu, \sigma^{2}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{1}{2 \pi \sigma^{2}}} \exp \left(-\frac{1}{2 \sigma^{2}}(x-\mu)^{2}\right)$
    • standard normal distribution with $\mu=0$ and $\sigma=1$
  • when evaluate the PDF, use $\beta \in(0, \infty)$ to control the precision
    • $\mathcal{N}\left(x ; \mu, \beta^{-1}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{\beta}{2 \pi}} \exp \left(-\frac{1}{2} \beta(x-\mu)^{2}\right)$
  • a good default choice for two major reasons:
    • First, many distributions we wish to model are truly close to being normal distributions.
    • Second, out of all possible probability distributions with the same variance, the normal distribution encodes the maximum amount of uncertainty over the real numbers.
      • inserts the least amount of prior knowledge into a model.
  • multivariate normal distribution, with a positive definite symmetric matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, The parameter $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ gives the covariance matrix of the distribution.:
    • $\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{x} ; \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{\Sigma})=\sqrt{\frac{1}{(2 \pi)^{n} \operatorname{det}(\mathbf{\Sigma})}} \exp\left( -\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top} \mathbf{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$
    • with precision matrix $\boldsymbol{\beta}$
      • $\mathcal{N}\left(\boldsymbol{x} ; \boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\beta}^{-1}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{\operatorname{det}(\boldsymbol{\beta})}{(2 \pi)^{n}}} \exp \left(-\frac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})^{\top} \boldsymbol{\beta}(\boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{\mu})\right)$

3.9.4 Exponential and Laplace Distributions

  • we often want to have a probability distribution with a sharp point 边界点 at $x = 0$.
  • we can use the exponential distribution:
    • $p(x ; \lambda)=\lambda \mathbf{1}_{x \geq 0} \exp (-\lambda x)$
    • indicator function $\mathbf{1}_{x \geq 0}$ to assign probability zero to all negative values of $x$
  • Laplace distribution allows us to place a sharp peak of probability mass at an arbitrary point $\mu$ is the
    • $\operatorname{Laplace}(x ; \mu, \gamma)=\frac{1}{2 \gamma} \exp \left(-\frac{|x-\mu|}{\gamma}\right)$

3.9.5 The Dirac Distribution and Empirical Distribution

  • Dirac delta function, $\delta(x)$, which is called a generalized function that is defined in terms of its properties when integrated.
    • $p(x)=\delta(x-\mu)$
  • A common use of the Dirac delta distribution is as a component of an empirical distribution,
    • $\hat{p}(\boldsymbol{x})=\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \delta\left(\boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}\right)$

3.9.6 Mixtures of Distributions

  • A mixture distribution is made up of several component distributions.
  • A very powerful and common type of mixture model is the Gaussian mixture, in which the components $p(\mathbf{x} | c=i)$ are Gaussians.
    • In addition to the means and covariances, the parameters of a Gaussian mixture specify the prior probability $\alpha_{i}=P(c=i)$ given to each component $i$ .
      • The word prior indicates that it expresses the model’s beliefs about $c$ before it has observed.
      • $P( c \ |\ \boldsymbol{x} )$ is a posterior probability , because it is computed after observation of $\mathrm{x}$.
  • A Gaussian mixture model is a universal approximator of densities, in the sense that any smooth density can be approximated with any specific nonzero amount of error by a Gaussian mixture model with enough components.

3.10 Useful Properties of Common Functions

  • logistic sigmoid
    • $\sigma(x)=\frac{1}{1+\exp (-x)}$
    • The logistic sigmoid is commonly used to produce the $\phi$ parameter of a Bernoulli distribution because its range is $(0, 1)$.
    • The sigmoid function saturates when its argument is very positive or very negative, meaning that the function becomes very flat and insensitive to small changes in its input.
  • softplus function
    • $\zeta(x)=\log (1+\exp (x))$
    • The softplus function can be useful for producing the $\beta$ or $\sigma$ parameter of a normal distribution because its range is $(0, \infty)$.
    • also arises commonly when manipulating expressions involving sigmoids.
    • The name of the softplus function comes from the fact that it is a smoothed, or “softened,” version of positive part function
      • $x^{+}=\max (0, x)$
  • Useful Properties to memorize
    • $\sigma(x)=\frac{\exp (x)}{\exp (x)+\exp (0)}$
    • $\frac{d}{d x} \sigma(x)=\sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$
    • $1-\sigma(x)=\sigma(-x)$
    • $\log \sigma(x)=-\zeta(-x)$
    • $\frac{d}{d x} \zeta(x)=\sigma(x)$
    • $\forall x \in(0,1), \sigma^{-1}(x)=\log \left(\frac{x}{1-x}\right)$
    • $\forall x>0, \zeta^{-1}(x)=\log (\exp (x)-1)$
    • $\zeta(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{x} \sigma(y) d y$
    • $\zeta(x)-\zeta(-x)=x$
  • logit, $\sigma^{-1}(x)$
  • $\zeta(x)-\zeta(-x)=x$ shows just as $x$ can be recovered from its positive part function and its negative part function via the identity $x^{+}−x^{-} = x$ , it is also possible to recover $x$ using the same relationship between $\zeta(x)$ and $\zeta(-x)$,

3.11 Bayes’ Rule

  • $P(\mathrm{x} | \mathrm{y})=\frac{P(\mathrm{x}) P(\mathrm{y} | \mathrm{x})}{P(\mathrm{y})}$
  • it is usually feasible to compute $P(\mathrm{y})=\sum_{x} P(\mathrm{y} | x) P(x)$, so we do not need to begin with knowledge of $P(\mathrm{y})$

3.12 Technical Details of Continuous Variables

  • A proper formal understanding of continuous random variables and probability density functions requires measure theory 测度论.
    • measure zero, a rigorous way of describing that a set of points is negligibly small.
    • almost everywhere, A property that holds almost everywhere holds throughout all space except for on a set of measure zero.
  • Another technical detail of continuous variables relates to handling continuous random variables that are deterministic functions of one another.
    • need to account the distortion of space introduced by the function
      • which means the different $\delta \boldsymbol{x}$ and $\delta \boldsymbol{y}$

3.13 Information Theory

  • It was originally invented to study sending messages from discrete alphabets over a noisy channel, such as communication via radio transmission.
  • information theory tells how to design optimal codes and calculate the expected length of messages sampled from specific probability distributions using various encoding schemes.
  • In this textbook, we mostly use a few key ideas from information theory to characterize probability distributions or to quantify similarity between probability distributions.
  • The basic intuition behind information theory is that learning that an unlikely event has occurred is more informative than learning that a likely event has occurred.
    • Likely events should have low information content
    • Less likely events should have higher information content
    • Independent events should have additive information
  • self-information for an event $\mathrm{x} = x$
    • $I(x)=-\log P(x)$
    • with base $e$, units: nats
      • One nat is the amount of information gained by observing an event of probability $\frac{1}{e}$
    • with base $2$, units called bits or shannons
      • just a rescaling of information measured in nats
  • Shannon entropy: quantify the amount of uncertainty in an entire probability distribution
    • $H(\mathrm{x})=\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim P}[I(x)]=-\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim P}[\log P(x)]$
    • also denoted $H(P )$
  • If we have two separate probability distributions $P(x )$ and $Q(x )$ over the same random variable $x$ , we can measure how different these two distributions are using the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence:
    • $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P | Q)=\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim P}\left[\log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\right]=\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim P}[\log P(x)-\log Q(x)]$
    • In the case of discrete variables, it is the extra amount of information needed to send a message containing symbols drawn from probability distribution $P$, when we use a code that was designed to minimize the length of messages drawn from probability distribution $Q$ .
    • sometimes not symmetric
      • $D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P | Q) \neq D_{\mathrm{KL}}(Q | P)$
  • cross-entropy
    • $H(P, Q)=H(P)+D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P | Q)$
    • $H(P, Q)=-\mathbb{E}_{\mathrm{x} \sim P} \log Q(x)$
    • we treat $0 \log 0$ as $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x \log x=0$

3.14 Structured Probabilistic Models

  • Using a single function to describe the entire joint probability distribution can be very inefficient.
  • we can split a probability distribution into many factors that we multiply together.
  • We can greatly reduce the cost of representing a distribution if we are able to find a factorization into distributions over fewer variables.
    • Each factor uses a number of parameters that is exponential in the number of variables in the factor.
  • graph, in the sense of graph theory:
    • a set of vertices that may be connected to each other with edges.
  • When we represent the factorization of a probability distribution with a graph, we call it a structured probabilistic model, or graphical model.
    • two main kinds of structured probabilistic models:
      • Directed models use graphs with directed edges, and they represent factorizations into conditional probability distributions, as in the example above.
        • Specifically, a directed model contains one factor for every random variable $\mathrm{x}_{i}$ in the distribution, and that factor consists of the conditional distribution over $\mathrm{x}_{i}$ given the parents of $\mathrm{x}_{i}$ , denoted $Pa_{\mathcal{G}}\left(\mathrm{x}_{i}\right)$:
        • $p(\mathbf{x})=\prod_{i} p\left(\mathrm{x}_{i} | P a_{\mathcal{G}}\left(\mathrm{x}_{i}\right)\right)$
        • e.g.
          • $p(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}, \mathrm{e})=p(\mathrm{a}) p(\mathrm{b} | \mathrm{a}) p(\mathrm{c} | \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}) p(\mathrm{d} | \mathrm{b}) p(\mathrm{e} | \mathrm{c})$
      • Undirected models use graphs with undirected edges, and they represent factorizations into a set of functions; unlike in the directed case, these functions are usually not probability distributions of any kind.
        • Any set of nodes that are all connected to each other in $\mathcal{G}$ is called a clique 团.
          • Each clique $\mathcal{C}^{(i)}$ in an undirected model is associated with a factor $\phi^{(i)}\left(\mathcal{C}^{(i)}\right)$. These factors are just functions.
          • The output of each factor must be non-negative, but no constraint that the factor must sum or integrate to $1$.
          • The probability of a configuration of random variables is proportional to the product of all these factors
          • normalization by a constant $Z$
            • defined to be the sum or integral over all states of the product of the $\phi$ functions
        • $p(\mathbf{x})=\frac{1}{Z} \prod_{i} \phi^{(i)}\left(\mathcal{C}^{(i)}\right)$
        • e.g.
          • $p(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}, \mathrm{d}, \mathrm{e})=\frac{1}{Z} \phi^{(1)}(\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}) \phi^{(2)}(\mathrm{b}, \mathrm{d}) \phi^{(3)}(\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{e})$
  • Being directed or undirected is not a property of a probability distribution; it is a property of a particular description of a probability distribution, but any probability distribution may be described in both ways.
entry.summary: The notes of deep learning book. Chapter 3.

entry.title: DL-book 2 Linear Algebra
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

2.1 Scalars, Vectors, Matrices and Tensors

2.2 Multiplying Matrices and Vectors

  • matrix product
    • $\boldsymbol{C}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B}$
    • $C_{i, j}=\sum_{k} A_{i, k} B_{k, j}$
  • element-wise product
    • $\boldsymbol{A} \odot \boldsymbol{B}$
  • dot product
    • $\boldsymbol{x} \cdot \boldsymbol{y} =\boldsymbol{x}^T \boldsymbol{y}$

2.3 Identity and Inverse Matrices 单位矩阵

  • identity matrix
    • $\boldsymbol{I}_{n} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$
    • $\forall \boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}, \boldsymbol{I}_{n} \boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{x}$
  • matrix inverse
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} \boldsymbol{A}=\boldsymbol{I}_{n}$

2.4 Linear Dependence and Span 线性相关与生成子空间

  • The span of a set of vectors is the set of all points obtainable by linear combination of the original vectors.
  • Determining whether $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{b}$ has a solution thus amounts to testing whether b is in the span of the columns of $\boldsymbol{A}$.
    • This particular span is known as the column space 列空间, or the range 值域, of $\boldsymbol{A}$.
  • A set of vectors is linearly independent if no vector in the set is a linear combination of the other vectors.
  • Together, this means that the matrix must be square , that is, we require that m = n and that all the columns be linearly independent.
  • A square matrix with linearly dependent columns is known as singular.

2.5 Norms

  • $L^p$ norm
    • $|\boldsymbol{x}|_{p}=\left(\sum_{i}\left|x_{i}\right|^{p}\right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$
    • for $p \in \mathbb{R}, p \geq 1$
  • Norms, including the $L^p$ norm, are functions mapping vectors to non-negative values.
    • On an intuitive level, the norm of a vector $x$ measures the distance from the origin to the point $x$ .
  • More rigorously, a norm is any function $f$ that satisfies the following properties:
    • $f(\boldsymbol{x})=0 \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{x}=\mathbf{0}$
    • $f(\boldsymbol{x}+\boldsymbol{y}) \leq f(\boldsymbol{x})+f(\boldsymbol{y})$, (the triangle inequality)
    • $\forall \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, f(\alpha \boldsymbol{x})=|\alpha| f(\boldsymbol{x})$
  • $L^2$ norm, Euclidean norm
    • $|\boldsymbol{x}|$
    • with the subscript 2 omitted
  • squared $L^2$ norm
    • $\boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}$
    • is more convenient to work with mathematically and computationally
      • each derivative of the squared L2 norm wrt. each element of x depends only on the corresponding element of x , while all the derivatives of the L2 norm depend on the entire vector.
    • but the squared L2 norm increases very slowly near the origin.
  • $L^1$ norm
    • $|x|_{1}=\sum_{i}\left|x_{i}\right|$
  • $L^\infty$ norm, max norm
    • $|\boldsymbol{x}|_{\infty}=\max _{i}\left|x_{i}\right|$
      • the absolute value of the element with the largest magnitude in the vector
  • Frobenius norm
    • measure the size of a matrix
    • $|\boldsymbol{A}|_{F}=\sqrt{\sum_{i, j} A_{i, j}^{2}}$
    • $|\boldsymbol{A}|_{F}=\sqrt{\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}\right)}$

2.6 Special Kinds of Matrices and Vectors

  • Diagonal matrices
  • symmetric matrix
  • unit vector
  • orthonormal
    • If the vectors not only are orthogonal but also have unit norm, we call them orthonormal.
  • orthogonal matrix
    • An orthogonal matrix is a square matrix whose rows are mutually orthonormal and whose columns are mutually orthonormal:
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{\top} \boldsymbol{A}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}=\boldsymbol{I}$
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}=\boldsymbol{A}^{\top}$
    • so orthogonal matrices are of interest because their inverse is very cheap to compute.

2.7 Eigendecomposition

  • $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{v}=\lambda \boldsymbol{v}$
    • An eigenvector of a square matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ is a nonzero vector $\boldsymbol{v}$ such that multiplication by alters only the scale of $\boldsymbol{v}$. The scalar $\lambda$ is known as the eigenvalue corresponding to this eigenvector. The scalar $\lambda$ is known as the eigenvalue corresponding to this eigenvector.
    • we usually look only for unit eigenvectors.
  • in this book, we usually need to decompose only a specific class of matrices that have a simple decomposition
    • $\boldsymbol{A}=\boldsymbol{Q} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \boldsymbol{Q}^{\top}$

2.8 Singular Value Decomposition

  • The singular value decomposition (SVD) factorize a matrix into singular vectors and singular values .
  • $\boldsymbol{A}=\boldsymbol{U} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{V}^{\top}$
    • The matrices $\boldsymbol{U}$ and $\boldsymbol{V}$ are both defined to be orthogonal matrices. The matrix $\boldsymbol{D}$ is defined to be a diagonal matrix.
    • The left-singular vectors of $\boldsymbol{A}$ are the eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}$ . The right-singular vectors of $\boldsymbol{A}$ are the eigenvectors of $\boldsymbol{A}^{\top} \boldsymbol{A}$. The nonzero singular values of $\boldsymbol{A}$ are the square roots of the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{A}^{\top} \boldsymbol{A}$. The same is true for $\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}$.
  • we can use it to partially generalize matrix inversion to nonsquare matrices

2.9 The Moore-Penrose Pseudoinverse 伪逆

  • The pseudoinverse of $\boldsymbol{A}$ is defined as a matrix
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{+}=\lim _{\alpha \searrow 0}\left(\boldsymbol{A}^{\top} \boldsymbol{A}+\alpha \boldsymbol{I}\right)^{-1} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}$
  • Practical algorithms for computing
    • $\boldsymbol{A}^{+}=\boldsymbol{V} \boldsymbol{D}^{+} \boldsymbol{U}^{\top}$
    • the pseudoinverse $\boldsymbol{D}^{+}$ of a diagonal matrix $\boldsymbol{D}$ is obtained by taking the reciprocal of its nonzero elements then taking the transpose 非零元素求倒数再转置 of the resulting matrix.
  • the solution $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{A}^{+} \boldsymbol{y}$ with minimal Euclidean norm $|x|_{2}$ among all possible solutions.

2.10 The Trace Operator

  • gives the sum of all the diagonal entries of a matrix:
    • $\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A})=\sum_{i} \boldsymbol{A}_{i, i}$
  • Frobenius norm of a matrix:
    • $|A|_{F}=\sqrt{\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{A}^{\top}\right)}$
  • the trace operator is invariant to the transpose operator.
    • $\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A})=\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{A}^{\top}\right)$
  • The trace of a square matrix
    • $\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A B C})=\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{C A B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{B C A})$
    • $\operatorname{Tr}\left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} \boldsymbol{F}^{(i)}\right)=\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{F}^{(n)} \prod_{i=1}^{n-1} \boldsymbol{F}^{(i)}\right)$
  • for $\boldsymbol{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and $\boldsymbol{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$
    • $\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{B})=\operatorname{Tr}(\boldsymbol{B} \boldsymbol{A})$

2.11 The Determinant

  • $\det(\boldsymbol{A})$
  • The determinant is equal to the product of all the eigenvalues of the matrix.

2.12 Example: Principal Components Analysis

  • Suppose we have a collection of pointsm ${\boldsymbol{x}^{(1)}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{x}^{(m)}}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ and we want to apply lossy compression to these points.
  • For each point $\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ we will find a corresponding code vector $\boldsymbol{c}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{l}$.
    • $l$ is smaller than $n$
      • We will want to find some encoding function that produces the code for an input, $f(\boldsymbol{x}) = \boldsymbol{c}$ ,
      • and a decoding function that produces the reconstructed input given its code, $\boldsymbol{x} \approx g ( f( \boldsymbol{x})).$
  • PCA is defined by our choice of the decoding function.
    • we choose to use matrix multiplication to map the code back into $\mathbb{R}^{n}$.
    • Let $g(\boldsymbol{c})=\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c}$, where $\boldsymbol{D} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times l}$ is the matrix defining the decoding.
      • to simplify, PCA constrains the columns of $\boldsymbol{D}$ to be orthogonal to each other.
      • To give the problem a unique solution, we constrain all the columns of $\boldsymbol{D}$ to have unit norm.
    • the first thing we need to do is figure out how to generate the optimal code point $\boldsymbol{c}^{}$ for *each input point $\boldsymbol{x}$ .
      • minimize the distance between the input point $\boldsymbol{x}$ and its reconstruction, $g(\boldsymbol{c}^{*})$ .
        • $\boldsymbol{c}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{c}}{\arg \min }|\boldsymbol{x}-g(\boldsymbol{c})|_{2}$
        • switch to the squared L2 norm instead of using the L2 norm
          • $\boldsymbol{c}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{c}}{\arg \min }|\boldsymbol{x}-g(\boldsymbol{c})|_{2}^2$
        • The function being minimized simplifies to
          • $(\boldsymbol{x}-g(\boldsymbol{c}))^{\top}(\boldsymbol{x}-g(\boldsymbol{c}))$
          • $=\boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}-\boldsymbol{x}^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})-g(\boldsymbol{c})^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}+g(\boldsymbol{c})^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})$
          • $=\boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}-2\boldsymbol{x}^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})+g(\boldsymbol{c})^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})$
          • omit the first term, since this term does not depend on $\boldsymbol{c}$
        • $\boldsymbol{c}^{*}=\underset{c}{\arg \min }-2 \boldsymbol{x}^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})+g(\boldsymbol{c})^{\top} g(\boldsymbol{c})$
        • substitute in the definition of $g(\boldsymbol{c})$
          • $\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{c}^{*} &=\underset{c}{\arg \min }-2 \boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c}+\boldsymbol{c}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c} \ &=\underset{c}{\arg \min }-2 \boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c}+\boldsymbol{c}^{\top} \boldsymbol{I}_{l} \boldsymbol{c} \&=\underset{c}{\arg \min }-2 \boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c}+\boldsymbol{c}^{\top} \boldsymbol{c} \end{aligned}$
          • $\nabla_{c}\left(-2 \boldsymbol{x}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{c}+\boldsymbol{c}^{\top} \boldsymbol{c}\right)=\mathbf{0}$
            • $-2 \boldsymbol{D}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}+2 \boldsymbol{c}=\mathbf{0}$
            • $\boldsymbol{c}=\boldsymbol{D}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}$
      • This makes the algorithm efficient: we can optimally encode x using just a matrix-vector operation.
      • we can also define the PCA reconstruction operation:
        • $r(\boldsymbol{x})=g(f(\boldsymbol{x}))=\boldsymbol{D} \boldsymbol{D}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}$
    • Next, we need to choose the encoding matrix $\boldsymbol{D}$ .
      • Instead, we must minimize the Frobenius norm of the matrix of errors computed over all dimensions and all points:
      • $\boldsymbol{D}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{D}}{\arg \min } \sqrt{\sum_{i, j}\left(x_{j}^{(i)}-r\left(\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}\right)_{j}\right)^{2}}$ subject to $\boldsymbol{D}^{\top} \boldsymbol{D}=\boldsymbol{I}_{l}$
        • start by considering the case where $l = 1$
        • $\boldsymbol{d}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min } \sum_{i}\left|\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}-\boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}\right|_{2}^{2}$ subject to $|\boldsymbol{d}|_{2}=1$
        • $\boldsymbol{d}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min } \sum_{i}\left|\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}-\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}\boldsymbol{d} \right|_{2}^{2}$ subject to $|\boldsymbol{d}|_{2}=1$
        • $\boldsymbol{d}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min } \sum_{i}\left|\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)}-\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)\top} \boldsymbol{d}\boldsymbol{d} \right|_{2}^{2}$ subject to $|\boldsymbol{d}|_{2}=1$
        • $\boldsymbol{d}^{*}=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min }\left|\boldsymbol{X}-\boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right|_{F}^{2}$ subject to $\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{d}=1$
          • $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}, \boldsymbol{X}_{i, :}=\boldsymbol{x}^{(i)^{\top}}$
      • Disregarding the constraint for the moment, we can simplify the Frobenius norm portion as follows:
        • $\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min }\left|\boldsymbol{X}-\boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right|_{F}^{2}$
          • $=\underset{d}{\arg \min } \operatorname{Tr}\left(\left(\boldsymbol{X}-\boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)^{\top}\left(\boldsymbol{X}-\boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)\right)$
          • $=\underset{d}{\arg \min } \operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X}-\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}-d \boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X}+d \boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)$
          • $=\underset{d}{\arg \min }-2 \operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)+\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)$
          • $=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \min }-2 \operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)+\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d} \boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)$ subject to $\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{d}=1$
          • $=\underset{d}{\arg \min }-\operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d}\boldsymbol{d}^{\top}\right)$ subject to $\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{d}=1$
          • $=\underset{\boldsymbol{d}}{\arg \max } \operatorname{Tr}\left(\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X}^{\top} \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{d}\right)$ subject to $\boldsymbol{d}^{\top} \boldsymbol{d}=1$
      • $\lambda^n+(a{11}+a{22}+\cdots+a{nn})\lambda^{n-1}+a{11}a{22}\cdots{a{nn}}+\cdots=0$
entry.summary: The notes of deep learning book. Chapter 2.

entry.title: DL-book 1 Introduction
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

  • The difficulties faced by systems relying on hard-coded knowledge suggest that AI systems need the ability to acquire their own knowledge, by extracting patterns from raw data. This capability is known as machine learning.
  • A simple machine learning algorithm called logistic regression can determine whether to recommend cesarean delivery (Mor-Yosef 1990 et al., ).
    • uterine scar
    • Logistic regression learns how each of these features of the patient correlates with various outcomes.
  • A simple machine learning algorithm called naive Bayes can separate legitimate e-mail from spam e-mail.
  • The performance of these simple machine learning algorithms depends heavily on the representation of the data they are given.
  • Each piece of information included in the representation of the patient is known as a feature.
  • In computer science, operations such as searching a collection of data can proceed exponentially faster if the collection is structured and indexed intelligently.
  • One solution to this problem is to use machine learning to discover not only the mapping from representation to output but also the representation itself. This approach is known as representation learning.
  • The quintessential example of a representation learning algorithm is the autoencoder. An autoencoder is the combination of an encoder function that converts the input data into a diﬀerent representation, and a decoder function that converts the new representation back into the original format.
  • When designing features or algorithms for learning features, our goal is usually to separate the factors of variation that explain the observed data.
    • Instead, they may exist either as unobserved objects or unobserved forces in the physical world that aﬀect observable quantities.
    • A major source of diﬃculty in many real-world artiﬁcial intelligence applications is that many of the factors of variation inﬂuence every single piece of data we are able to observe.
    • Most applications require us to the factors of variation and discard the disentangle ones that we do not care about.
  • Deep learning solves this central problem in representation learning by introducing representations that are expressed in terms of other, simpler representations.
  • The quintessential example of a deep learning model is the feedforward deep network or multilayer perceptron (MLP).
    • We can think of each application of a diﬀerent mathematical function as providing a new representation of the input.
  • There are two main ways of measuring the depth of a model.
    • The ﬁrst view is based on the number of sequential instructions that must be executed to evaluate the architecture.
    • Another approach, used by deep probabilistic models, regards the depth of a model as being not the depth of the computational graph but the depth of the graph describing how concepts are related to each other.

1.1 Who Should Read This Book?

1.2 Historical Trends in Deep Learning

  • Broadly speaking, there have been three waves of development of deep learning:
    • deep learning known as cybernetics in the 1940s–1960s,
      • The earliest predecessors of modern deep learning were simple linear models motivated from a neuroscientiﬁc perspective.
        • $f(\boldsymbol{x}, \boldsymbol{w})=x_{1} w_{1}+\cdots+x_{n} w_{n}$
        • The adaptive linear element (ADALINE), which dates from about the same time, simply returned the value of $f(x)$ itself to predict a real number, and could also learn to predict these numbers from data.
        • Models based on the f(x w , ) used b the perceptron and ADALINE are called linear model.
        • The training algorithm used to adapt the weights of the ADALINE as a special case of an algorithm called stochastic gradient descent.
        • Linear models have many limitations. Most famously, they cannot learn the XOR function, where $f ([0,1],w) = 1$
          • This was the ﬁrst major dip in the popularity of neural networks.
        • Most neural networks today are based on a model neuron called the rectified linear unit .
        • The field of deep learning is primarily concerned with how to build computer systems that are able to successfully solve tasks requiring intelligence, while the field of computational neuroscience is primarily concerned with building more accurate models of how the brain actually works.
    • deep learning known as connectionism or parallel distributed processing in the 1980s–1990s,
      • The central idea in connectionism is that a large number of simple computational units can achieve intelligent behavior when networked together.
        • One of these concepts is that of distributed representation. This is the idea that each input to a system should be represented by many features, and each feature should be involved in the representation of many possible inputs.
        • Another major accomplishment of the connectionist movement was the successful use of back-propagation to train deep neural networks with internal representations and the popularization of the back-propagation algorithm
    • and the current resurgence under the name deep learning beginning in 2006.
      • Geoffrey Hinton showed that a kind of neural network called a deep belief network could be efficiently trained using a strategy called greedy layer-wise pretraining .
  • The neural perspective on deep learning is motivated by two main ideas.
    • One idea is that the brain provides a proof by example that intelligent behavior is possible, and a conceptually straightforward path to building intelligence is to reverse engineer the computational principles behind the brain and duplicate its functionality.
    • Another perspective is that it would be deeply interesting to understand the brain and the principles that underlie human intelligence, so machine learning models that shed light on these basic scientiﬁc questions are useful apart from their ability to solve engineering applications.
  • The modern term “deep learning” goes beyond the neuroscientiﬁc perspective on the current breed of machine learning models.
    • It appeals to a more general principle of learning multiple levels of composition, which can be applied in machine learning frameworks that are not necessarily neurally inspired.
  • As of 2016, a rough rule of thumb is that a supervised deep learning algorithm will generally achieve acceptable performance with around 5,000 labeled examples per category and will match or exceed human performance when trained with a dataset containing at least 10 million labeled examples.
  • Increasing Dataset Sizes + Increasing Model Sizes
entry.summary: The notes of deep learning book. Chapter 1.

entry.title: 工具 - Notion, Trello, OneNote等效率工具几句话对比 2019
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。

偶然间，看到各类文章对效率工具的吹捧。又很好奇，其中有些我又确实没有用过，就都试了一试。不如把我测试的感受跟大家分享一下吧！

我是客观中立的学生党。

0 使用场景

我的使用场景是：Android手机，iPad，Windows计算机，三个都要使用，这也是直接扔了印象笔记（免费版支持2个设备）不考虑的原因。

对，所有产品基于免费版本。

1 笔记本之王 OneNote

Notability当然是不可错过的好工具，我也一直在用。但是它没有win版本，所以导致我不能直接在win上桌面化操作。

没有什么笔记工具能与OneNote比肩了吧，5个G的免费容量也是够够的了。

  • 平台：Win下我比较喜欢用UWP版本
  • 优点
    • 全面、均衡的好
    • 大厂出品，前景美好
  • 缺点
    • 原生不支持Markdown不知道算不算一个小遗憾，因为对于我，我比较喜欢写md

2 模板笔记大师 Notion

各类平台无脑吹的最多的一款看似“全能”的笔记应用。花里胡哨。

我把他留着使用的原因是他丰富的模板，当我要在OneNote里写类似笔记时，我会去参考一下它的模板内容。

我一直不太推荐这种所谓的“全能型”工具，除非你是苹果、微软这种有强大的各方面能力支撑的公司，不然可能还是专心干一件事会比较好。

  • 优点
    • 模板丰富，丰富到感动你
    • 可选block 插件丰富，可以做出漂亮的、一体的笔记
  • 缺点
    • 慢。无形之中的慢，总感觉卡卡的
    • 花里胡哨，到处都是block和操作按钮。有一种富文本之于markdown的感觉
    • 免费版1000 blocks限制
    • 被墙风险，毕竟可以分享，有不可控风险

3 行事规划 Trello，Teambition

3.1 Trello

这一波试用后，留在我电脑上的应用。推荐。

这是一个逻辑简单的项目规划类应用，而我将其作为我的个人规划应用。我最近用它来做找工作和写毕设的规划。

如果你和我一样以前没用过，来看一看界面就知道这软件是怎么回事了。

  • 优点
    • 界面美观
    • 模板，尤其是个人项目逻辑友好
  • 缺点
    • 免费版的Power-Ups ，也就是原生插件，只支持一个，但是够用了
    • 被墙风险，不够这类应用也不用担心这个过多，墙了就转回Teambition

3.2 国产计划行事 Teambition

Trello类似的应用，国内用户推荐，国外用户不推荐。

  • 优点
    • 国产，被墙风险小
    • 本地支持
  • 缺点
    • 国内服务器对国外用户有点慢，账号又和国际服务器不通，导致随时登出，然后报个json的错误出来。就卸载了
    • 手机上操作逻辑我不太喜欢。为什么一屏就显示一个card？

4 大纲笔记：Dynalist，幕布

4.1 Dynalist

Markdown用户的笔记应用首选，比较完美的一款产品。需要搭配一个图床，我一直在用。

  • 优点
    • 华人产品
  • 缺点
    • 不支持代码块，这个要加上了就完美了
    • 文档多了后，树状结构看起来很乱

4.2 幕布

总体逻辑和Dynalist一致，有其独特优点。

  • 优点
    • 转换笔记到脑图
  • 缺点
    • 不支持Latex，不支持Latex公式必然是个巨大的败笔

5 定时任务：To-Do，奇妙清单

5.1 To-Do

奇妙清单的微软版。无脑吹微软的产品，大厂的软件生命值得期待、值得信赖。

  • 优点
    • 清爽，免费，前景好
    • 和微软产品的逐渐耦合
  • 缺点
    • 应该都是暂时的，比如iPad还没有Widget

5.2 奇妙清单

毫无疑问，奇妙清单在这类应用中几乎无懈可击。但从商业角度上看，我认为To-Do会逐渐吸收奇妙清单的优点，并被微软优先支持。

  • 优点
    • 完美
  • 缺点
    • 被大厂收购了，前景没有To-Do好

6 计算器 Uno Calculator

彩蛋推荐。

该计算器是基于Windows默认计算器的开源代码创建，Android和iPad都有下载。好用，四舍五入的看成是微软出品吧。

最后，欢迎读者指正，也期待您给我推荐更好用的工具。
entry.summary: 偶然间，看到各类文章对效率工具的吹捧。又很好奇，其中有些我又确实没有用过，就都试了一试。不如把我测试的感受跟大家分享一下吧！

entry.title: Robotics - Mensch-Maschine Interaktion Kurs
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

2018WS in TU Ilmenau
TOC of the course

Intro – Einführung

  • Organisatorisches
  • Begriffsbestimmung
  • Anwendungsgebiete
  • Teilbereiche
  • Literatur

A – Ausgewählte Basisoperationen für viele Erkennungsverfahren

1 Basisoperationen

  • Vorverarbeitung
    • mache Konzepten
    • Merkmalsextraktion
    • Klassifikation / Funktionsapproximation (Regression)/ Clusterung

2 Bildvorverarbeitung

  • Histogrammausgleich
    • Beleuchtungsausgleich
    • Histogrammausgleich
    • kumulative Histogramm
    • Summenhistogramm
  • Behandlung der Rand probleme
  • Auflösungspyramiden
    • Ortsfrequenzen
    • Abtasttheorem
  • Integralbilder A-28

3 Merkmalsextraktion

  • Hautfarberkennung
    • Farbraum
    • r-g space
    • Farbtemperatur
  • Gabor-Filterung A-34
    • Vorzugsrichtungen $\phi$
    • Vorzugsfrequenzen $\omega$
    • Kantendetektion
    • Jet
  • PCA A-52
    • Ablauf A-59
    • Dimension bestimmen
  • Linear subspace methods
    • steps A-63

4 Leistungsbewertung von Detektoren

  • Konfusionsmatrix
    • ACC/PPV/Recall/TNR/F1-score
  • ROC Curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic)
    • Diskrete Klassifikatoren
    • Einordnung aller Begrifflichkeiten A-78
    • AUC: Area Under Curve
      • ACC
      • BER
    • Precision-Recall-Curve A-82
      • F1-score
    • DET-Curve (Detection Error Tradeoff)
    • Mehrklassen-Bewertung
      • Problem Konfusionsmatrix A-89
    • CMC – Cumulative Match Characteristic

5 Analyse spatio-temporale Daten A-92

  • Dynamic Time Warping (DTW)
  • Hidden Markov Modelle (HMM) A-102

B – Wichtige Verfahren zur Erkennung von Nutzerzustand & Nutzerintention

1 Nutzerdetektion, Nutzertracking

  • Implizite Personendetektion mittels
    • Hintergrund-Modellierung B1-7
    • GMM - Gaussians Mixture Model
  • Explicit Methods
    • Monolithic
      • Person detection via faces, Template-based B1-13
        • Appearance-based
          • Distribution-based methods B1-18
          • Neural Networks
            • 什么形状匹配什么部位 B1-23
            • Bootstrapping B1-27
          • HMM B1-31
          • Boosting-basierte Klassifikatorkaskaden B1-35
            • Ensemble learning
            • Gesamtklassifikator (Strong Learner)
      • Full Body Models – Monolithic, Silhouette-based Person Detection
        • Ellipse, Parametric geometric models, Static contour models B1-48
        • Splines, 样条曲线, Point based B1-52
        • ASM, Active Shape Models, B1-58
      • Feature-based, B1-61
        • Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG)
          • Normalisierung, B1-69
      • Deep Learning
        • CNN
    • Part-based
      • Feature-based
        • Implicit Shape Model (ISM) B1-77
  • Personendetektion in 3D Tiefenbildern B1-80
    • Szenengeometrie
    • Speckle Muster B1-85
    • Monolithic
      • Datenbasis: Tiefenkarte
      • Tiefentemplates, B1-90
      • histogrammbasierten Features
        • HOD
        • Combo-HOD
        • HDD
    • Mehrteilig, B1-95
      • Datenbasis: Punktewolke
      • histogrammbasierten Features
        • HLSN
      • geometrischen und statistischen Features
        • Layered Approach
        • AdaBoost
  • Multi-Cue People Tracking, B1-103
    • Bayes-Filter
      • Continuous: Kalman filter
      • Discrete: Particle filter

2 Zeigeposen- und Gestenerkennung B2-1

  • Simple template matching approach
    • Dynamic Gesture Recognition, B2-11
  • Gesture recognition by statistical techniques (HMMs), B2-13

3 Nutzeridentifikation, B3-1

  • nicht-biometrische Widererkennung
    • markante Punkte
    • statische Merkmale
    • semantischer Attribute
  • biometrische Nutzeridentifikation anhand des Gesichts, B3-8
    • Elastic Graph Matching (EGM)

4 Erkennung von Mimik (Emotionen, Stress) und Interaktionsinteresse, B4-1

  • emotional classes
  • visual classes
    • Facial Action Coding Systems (FACS)
  • Comparison, B4-12
  • automatic facial expression analysis
    • Facial Data Acquisition
    • Facial Feature Extraction
      • Deformation Extraction
        • Image-based
        • model-based
          • Active Appearance Models (AAMs)
      • Movement Extraction

        C – Anwendungsbeispiele für Assistenzfunktionen

1 Sturzdetektion im häuslichen Umfeld

  • Tragbare Sensoren, C1—5
  • Bildbasierte Verfahren, C1—7
    • von Bewegungen
    • Detektion liegender Personen
  • NDT—Sturzdetektor

2 Soziale Assistenzroboter für die Gesundheitsassistenz (Rehabilitation)
entry.summary: Auf Deutsch verfasst. Die Verzeichnisstruktur des Kurses Mensch-Maschine Interaktion.

entry.title: Robotics - Robotvision Kurs
entry.content:
http://xlindo.com
所有文章遵循CC BY-NC-SA 4.0许可协议。请按许可使用。
Blog content follows the CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 License.

2018WS in TU Ilmenau
TOC of the course

1 Intro, Begriffsklärung, Literatur, …

Weshalb Vision, 1-13

2 Neuronale Basisoperationen der visuo-motorischen

Verarbeitung – der neuronale Instruktionssatz

2.1 Funktionelle Abbildungen $R^n \to R^2$, 2-2

  • Faltungsoperatoren, 2-8
  • Ortsvariante Faltungsoperatoren
  • Gaborfilter, 2-17

2.2 Topographische Abbildungen $R^2 \to R^2$, 2-20

  • Retinotope Projektion
  • Komplexe Funktion, 2-28

2.3 Ortsvariante Informationsverarbeitung

2.4 Auflösungspyramiden, Kap.3.1

2.5 Topologisch kodierte neuronale Felder im $R^m$

2.6 Neuronale Felddynamiken, 2-45

  • AMARI-Selektionsdynamik

2.7 Geschichtete 2D-Repräsentationen, 2-54

2.8 Randprobleme, 2-58

3 Basisoperationen & Technologien für die visuelle, Umgebungswahrnehmung

3.1 Detektoren & Deskriptoren für Landmarken und Interest-Points in 2D-Bildern

  • Vergleich & Zusammenfassung 3.1-68
  • Feature-Auswahl
  • -invarianz, 3.1-6
  • mit geringen Invarianzleistungen
    • Sobel, 3.1-10
    • Moravec, 3.1-11
    • Harris, Shi/Tomasi, 3.1-12
    • Patches (Korrelationsfenster)
  • mit Invarianzleistungen
    • SIFT, 3.1-22
      • Gauß‘sche Skalenraum, Gaußfilter, 3.1-27
      • DoG-Skalenraum, 3.1-34
        • Rotationsinvarianz
      • Extrema im DoG Skalenraum, 3.1-39
        • Skalierungsinvarianz
      • Genaue Lokalisierung der Keypoints, 3.1-42
      • Herausfiltern von Kanten & „Low-contrast“, 3.1-44
        • 错误选择条纹的解决办法
      • Keypoint Orientierung， 3.1-46
        • Rotationsinvarianz
      • 小总结，3.1-48
      • Keypoint Descriptors
    • SURF, 3.1-56
      • DoG —> Hesse-Matrix
      • Integralbilder
    • FAST, 3.1-59
    • BRIEF, BRISK, ORB, FREAK

3.2 Bewegungssehen und optischer Fluss, 3.2-1

  • Korrespondenzproblem, 3.2-5
  • OF-Verfahren
    • Feature-basiert, 3.1,3.4
    • Helligkeitsbasiert
      • Ortsbereich
        • korrelationsbasiert, 3.2-10
          • Alternativen zur Kreuzkorrelation, 3.2-13
          • Verarbeitungsschritte, 3.2-19
        • Differentielle Verfahren, 3.2-24
        • spärlicher/dichter Fluss 对比, 3.2-32
      • Frequenzbereich

3.3 Tiefenwahrnehmung, Tiefenkameras (RGB-D Kameras), 3.3-1

  • Passiv

    • Okulomotorische Tiefenhinweise
    • Monokulare Tiefenhinweise
      • Strukturinduziert
      • Bewegungsinduziert
    • Binokulare Tiefenwahrnehmung, 3.3-7
      • Stereovision
        • Binoculares visuelles Feld
        • Retinale Disparität
        • Horopter
      • Kameraanordnungen, 3.3-12
        • Parallel angeordnete Kameras
          • Epipolarlinie
        • Kameras mit Fixationspunkt
      • Kamerakalibrierung, 3.3-17
      • Korrespondenzanalyse, 3.3-18
        • Heuristiken für eine robuste Suche, 3.3-23
      • Verarbeitungsschritte zur Berechnung einer 2D-Tiefenkarte, 3.3-26
        • 1.Bildvorverarbeitung
        • 2.Korrespondenzanalyse (Erstellung eines Disparitätswürfel)
        • 3.Aggregation (innerhalb des Disparitätswürfels)
          • Membran Diffusion
        • 4.Disparitätsauswahl im Disparitätswürfel
          • Dynamischer Programmierung
        • 5.Tiefenberechnung und Nachbearbeitung der Tiefenkarte
          • mittels Triangulation, 3.3-37
    • Multiokulare Tiefeninformationen

  • Aktiv

    • strukturiertem Licht, 3.3-43
      • Graycode-Sequenz
      • Speckle Mustern, Kinect 1
    • Time-of-Flight Kameras, 3.3-51
      • calc
      • Kinect 2
  • Gegenüberstellung aktiver Verfahren zur 3D-Raumwahrnehmung, 3.3-61
  • Nutzung von Tiefeninformation, 3.3-62

3.4 Detektoren & Deskriptoren für Tiefenbilder (3D-Bilder), Features in 3D-Raumrepräsentationen, 3.4-1

  • Normal Aligned Radial Features (NARF), 3.4-4
  • SURE, 3.4-6
  • NDT-Map, 3.4-10

3.5 Visuelle Odometrie, 3.5-1

  • Kamerabild
  • Featureextraktion
    • 各种算子的对比
  • Tracking /Matching
  • Reduzierung von Ausreißern, 3.5-8
    • RANSAC
    • Smoothness Motion Constraint
    • Keyframes
  • Posenschätzung, 3.5-14
    • 5 oder 8 Punkte Algorithmus
    • Bündelausgleich

4 Vision-basierte Roboternavigation

4.1 Hindernisvermeidung, Lokale Navigation

  • Kartenfreie
    • Direkte Bilddaten
      • ALVINN, 4.1-6
    • Optischer Fluss
      • flussbasierter Balancing Strategien, 4.1-11
    • untergrundbasierter Verfahren, 4.1-12
    • Untergrund-Farbreferenz, 4.1-13
      • Problem, 4.1-21
      • multiplen Features, 4.1-25
    • Visual Sonar
  • Kartenbasierte
    • lokale visuelle Navigation, Untergrund-Hindernis-Karten, 4.1-34
      • Transformation der Koordinatensysteme
      • Behandlung der Grundfläche/Fahrebene
        • RANSAC, 4.1-38
      • Berechnung einer 3D Belegtheitskarte
      • Projektion der 3D-Daten in 2D-Ebene
      • Bewegungssteuerung, 4.1-47
        • Dynamic Window Approach, DWA
    • 2D / 3D Belegtheitskarten

4.2 Mapping und Selbstlokalisation

  • Bayes-Filter, 4.2-5
  • Monte Carlo Localization, 4.2-9
    • Partikel Filters
  • Ansichtsbasierte visuelle MCL, 4.2-18
  • MCL mit NDT-Karten (NDT – MCL), 4.2-28

4.3 Vision-based SLAM (Simult Localization &Map Building)

  • Systematisierung
    • Online SLAM, 4.3-4
      • RBPF-SLAM
    • Full SLAM
  • Graphbasiertes SLAM – ein Full-SLAM Verfahren
    • Prinzipielle Vorgehensweise, 4.3-11
    • Odometrie-, Matching- und Schleifenschluss-Kanten, 4.3-12
    • Map-Matching, 4.3-14
      • Iterative Closest Point (ICP) Algorithmus, 4.3-14
      • NDT-Karten
    • Schleifenschluss
    • Graphoptimierung, 4.3-21
  • Zusammenfassung, 4.3-27

5 Semantisches Szenenverständnis
